sessions.name: 5.A - Symposium: Wardens of Knowledge: Sleuths and Publishers Collaborating to Preserve Research Integrity
sessions.details:
In this session, publishers and research integrity "sleuths" will come together to discuss their current collaborative efforts within a complex ecosystem. We will share perspectives on the role of "sleuths" in multi-agency initiatives to protect research integrity, and provide case studies to highlight successful collaborations with publishers (30min). Additionally, we will address the challenges faced in working together and offer recommendations for how we may overcome these (30min). We will finally present an overview of how we envision ‘best practices’ to apply post-publication corrections to the scholarly record (15min). Dr Adya Misra is Associate Director, Research Integrity (UK) oversees all publication ethics and research integrity activity in Sage journals.
Ms Laura Wilson is Head of Research Integrity and Ethics (UK) leads a team of Research Integrity Managers in Taylor and Francis. Dr René Aquarius is a researcher at Radboud University Medical Centre (NL) who has authored several articles raising concerns about the integrity of the scholarly record and balances “sleuthing” with his neurosurgery research. Dr Achal Agarwal is a researcher and founder of India Research Watch (India). Achal has authored several articles highlighting the issues of research integrity and research evaluation. The conference theme highlights two of our biggest challenges in research integrity and both concerns cannot be addressed without collaboration between various stakeholders in the research ecosystem. Research Integrity sleuths are often left out of these collaborations; without a formal role or professionalized outputs, many of our sleuths are still operating in the shadows and treated as irritants by journals and publishers. This session aims to elevate the voices of sleuths and highlight instances where collaborative efforts have worked well, and address what remains to be done. The work of research integrity sleuths could be weaponised by forces that aim to disrupt knowledge and scholarship, so it is essential that their role as a firm stakeholder and partner is recognised by journals and publishers. TIME SCHEDULE 3:30pm - 3:40pm: Welcome and Introduction - Dr Adya Misra 3:40pm - 4:00pm: Perspectives on the role of "sleuths" in multi-agency initiatives - Dr Achal Agrawal 4:00pm - 4:20pm: Case studies to highlight successful collaborations with publishers - Dr Rene Aquarius 4:20pm - 4:40pm: Overview of how we envision ‘best practices’ to apply post-publication corrections - Ms. Laura Wilson 4:40pm - 5:00pm: Q&A

speakers.name: Rene Aquarius
speakers.title: Dr.
speakers.bio:
Rene Aquarius is a biomedical scientist working at the Neurosurgery department of the Radboud university medical center in Nijmegen, The Netherlands.

Besides his regular work - which focuses mainly on systematic reviews, as well as vivo, in vitro, and clinical research in the field of vascular neurosurgery -, he has also been active as a so-called ‘science sleuth’ for the last two years.

As a science sleuth, he is focusing mostly on finding image-related issues in scientific articles.

speakers.name: Adya Misra
speakers.title: Dr.
speakers.bio: Adya Misra is Associate Director, Research Integrity at Sage and maintains responsibility over publishing ethics activity in Sage’s 1500+ journals portfolio. Adya leads the Global Research Integrity Team at Sage responsible for the resolution of all publication ethics and research integrity issues raised in Sage journals. Through this work, Adya is responsible for the development of policies and cross-functional processes to support the effective resolution of publication ethics issues, with overarching aims to prevent publication of problematic content. Adya has previously worked in senior editorial roles at PLOS and PeerJ bringing together subject level knowledge in molecular biology, medicine and public health along with research integrity expertise. As a journal editor and researcher in previous roles, Adya has broad interests in science communication, peer review and understanding the drivers of change in the research landscape. Adya has a PhD in Mitochondrial genetics and completed postdoctoral training at Karolinska Institute, Stockholm and Institute for Cancer Research, London. Adya was awarded the People of Publishing award in 2024 to recognise her contributions to scholarly publishing.

speakers.name: Christopher Rickerby
speakers.title: Mr.
speakers.bio:
Christopher Rickerby

Data Integrity Analyst, EMBO Press

Christopher Rickerby is the Data Integrity Analyst for EMBO Press, responsible for ensuring image and data integrity across all journals, both pre- and post-publication. Since joining EMBO in 2018, he has developed and refined workflows to detect data manipulation, manage corrections, and strengthen transparency in scientific publishing.

A member of the STM Integrity Working Group, Chris collaborates internationally to advance best practices and evaluate emerging tools for research integrity, including AI-based systems designed to identify paper-milled and fraudulent submissions. His work supports the accuracy, reliability, and trustworthiness of EMBO Press publications.

speakers.name: Clayton Lewis
speakers.title: Dr.
speakers.bio: Clayton Lewis is the Manager of Outreach & Education in the Research Security Division at the University of Calgary. Previously he worked as a social policy researcher in municipal government. Clayton holds a PhD from York University where he conducted research on challenges to international liberal-democratic institutions as well as prospects and pathways for democratic renewal.

speakers.name: Reese Richardson
speakers.title: Dr.
speakers.bio: Reese Richardson is a postdoctoral research fellow in the Center for Science of Science and Innovation (CSSI) at the Kellogg School of Management, Northwestern University. Broadly, his research interests concern the formation of collective knowledge, maintaining the integrity of the scientific literature, and systematic studies of industrialized academic and scientific fraud. He earned his PhD in Interdisciplinary Biological Sciences at Northwestern University in 2024 in the laboratory of Luís A. Nunes Amaral. He is one of the maintainers of the Collection of Open Science Integrity Guides (COSIG, cosig.net), an open-source resource for performing post-publication peer review.

speakers.name: Yaryna Pikulytska
speakers.title: Dr.
speakers.bio:
A pediatrician and founder of the outpatient clinic "Vulyk," with a medical background
specializing in bioethics. Established the Institutional Review Board (IRB) at the Ukrainian
Catholic University and serves as a lecturer at its School of Bioethics. Passionate about
healthcare organization, pediatrics, family medicine, bioethics (particularly research ethics),
and evidence-based medicine and science.

speakers.name: Angela Seggio - Welch
speakers.title: Dr.
speakers.bio: Angela is the Operational Excellence Manager within Elsevier's Research Integrity and Publishing Ethics Center of Expertise.  She is part of the multi-disciplinary team working on research integrity signal development and deployment across our pre- and post-publication investigative units.  In her 15 years with Elsevier, Angela has experience as a Senior Publisher managing journal portfolios across physical and health & medical sciences, Team Leader overseeing scientific editors responsible for facilitating the article transfer service, and most recently, as the Policy and Guideline Manager reviewing and developing policies, guidelines, and curated training programs within the research integrity team.

speakers.name: Daniel Barr
speakers.title: Dr
speakers.bio: Dr Daniel Barr is the Principal Research Integrity Advisor at RMIT University, Australia, specialising in responsible research governance, management, and teaching. He contributes globally to research integrity through roles with the World Conferences on Research Integrity Foundation, the Asia-Pacific Research Integrity Network, and the Australian Academy of Science. Dr Barr co-authored the APEC Guiding Principles for Research Integrity and co-chaired the 6th World Conference on Research Integrity held in Hong Kong. In 2021, Dr Barr was awarded the Paul Taylor Medal for Excellence in Research Integrity by the Australasian Research Management Society.

speakers.name: Willie Koh
speakers.title: Dr.
speakers.bio: Dr Willie Koh is the Director of the Research Integrity & Ethics Office at the Nanyang Technological University (NTU), Singapore, where he leads initiatives in research governance and the proper conduct of research. He is dedicated to strengthening institutional frameworks that uphold research integrity, data management, and regulatory compliance, ensuring NTU remains at the forefront of responsible and trustworthy science. With a strong track record in shaping policies and guiding best practices, he plays a central role in supporting faculty, researchers, and students in building a culture of trust in research. Dr Koh’s background is in the life sciences, with a PhD in Virology from University College London and a B.Sc from the National University of Singapore.

speakers.name: James Heathers
speakers.title: Dr.
speakers.bio: I am the director of The Medical Evidence Project, a project of The Center for Scientific Integrity. The MEP aims to reduce harm to patients by finding and making known serious errors in the medical literature. The MEP is especially interested in detecting and shining light on errors arising from low-quality science and fraudulent work in areas that involve large numbers of patients. I have worked in forensic metascience for ten years. My  PhD in Computational Psychophysiology (Usyd)

speakers.name: Shiwei Liu
speakers.title: Ms.
speakers.bio: I am a PhD student in Information Sciences at the University of Illinois Urbana-Champaign (ScienceNLP Lab), where I develop natural language processing methods to extract, evaluate, and organize knowledge from scientific literature. My current work builds automated tools for assessments of randomized controlled trial (RCT) reporting with respect to CONSORT and SPIRIT guidelines, to help authors, editors, and reviewers identify omissions before publication and to strengthen the evidence base used in systematic reviews. More broadly, I study how AI can advance research integrity by improving transparency, reproducibility, and auditability of biomedical reporting.

speakers.name: Dora Pejdo
speakers.title: Miss
speakers.organization: University Of Split School Of Medicine
speakers.bio: I am a researcher and PhD student at the University of Split School of Medicine in Croatia. I graduated my Master of Science in Psychology at a university in the UK and then returned to Croatia, where I joined the Horizon Europe project iRISE: Improving Reproducibility in Science. My work focuses on understanding and improving research reproducibility and integrity across different scientific fields. I am now in the third year of my PhD studies, exploring open science practices and how different stakeholders can work together to strengthen research quality. Alongside my research, I teach at the Faculty of Humanities and Social Sciences, Department of Psychology, at the University of Split. I am passionate about improving my own scientific practice, supporting responsible research, and helping to build a culture of transparency and trust in science.

speakers.name: Jay Patel
speakers.title: Mr.
speakers.bio: As a Ph.D. candidate in Information Studies, I study communities and tools for evaluating research reports. I am inspired by problems in open science and metascience (the science of science) like scaling the critical evaluation of research and synthesizing research in a form capable of being directly relevant to laypeople and decision-makers. From Information Studies and HCI, I borrow user-centered thinking, design patterns, and technological solutions. Over the long term, I envision developing sociotechnical solutions across the research cycle that align our stated scholarly values with actual practices.

speakers.name: Mohammad Hosseini
speakers.title: Dr.
speakers.bio: Dr. Mohammad Hosseini is an assistant professor at Northwestern University Feinberg School of Medicine. His research is focused on ethical implications of using artificial intelligence (AI) in research and publications, research ethics and integrity and open science. He is involved in and leads various ethics projects in national and international organizations (e.g., Association of Professional and Practical Ethics, National Information Standards Organization, National Academy of Sciences, and Global Young Academy). He teaches research ethics and integrity and responsible conduct of research at Northwestern University and various international institutions.

I have served on several boards/committees in various capacities and collaborated with multiple institutions/teams on SRHR projects. My research outputs have contributed significantly to informing SRHR policy and program development in Ghana and beyond. I am currently an Associate Professor at the University of Education, Winneba, Ghana.

speakers.title: Dr.
speakers.organization: Finnish National Board On Research Integrity Tenk
speakers.bio: Petra Falin is a senior adviser at the Finnish National Board on Research Integrity TENK. In this role, she monitors and coordinates the work of the ethics committees in human and social sciences at the national level. She also participates in the other tasks of TENK in promoting and monitoring research integrity.

speakers.name: Natalie Evans
speakers.title: Dr
speakers.bio: Natalie Evans is an Associate Professor in the Department of Ethics, Law and Humanities at Amsterdam UMC. Her work explores how responsible research practices are conceptualized and embedded within academic communities, with a focus on virtue ethics, fairness, equity, and environmental responsibility in research conduct. She has played a leading role in major European research integrity projects, including RE4GREEN, PREPARED, AIOLIA, VIRT2UE, and EnTIRE. As Scientific Coordinator of VIRT2UE, she oversaw the development of a virtue ethics–based training program that prepared nearly 500 trainers and reached over 4,000 researchers across Europe. Through this work, she has also trained research integrity trainers from Europe, Africa, and North America. Natalie leads the Research Integrity Programme at Amsterdam UMC and is co-founder of the Embassy of Good Science, an international platform for sharing resources and fostering dialogue on research ethics and integrity.

speakers.name: Julia Gunn
speakers.title: Dr.
speakers.bio: Amy Flanders Milligan earned her PhD studying publishing history at the University of Oxford before taking up postdoctoral fellowships at New York University and the University of London. She then moved into scholarly publishing with roles at Oxford University Press and Taylor & Francis in digital archives and open research. She now works as the Associate Director for Research and Impact at the Said Business School, University of Oxford. She also serves the Association of Research Managers & Administrators (ARMA) UK as a champion of the Metaresearch Special Interest Group and on the editorial board of ARMA Insights magazine.

speakers.name: Cory Goldstein
speakers.title: Dr.
speakers.bio: Cory E. Goldstein is a Postdoctoral Fellow with the Methodological and Implementation Research Program at the Ottawa Hospital Research Institute and the School of Epidemiology and Public Health at the University of Ottawa. His research interests lie at the intersection of ethics and clinical trial design, with a focus on ethical issues raised by pragmatic randomized controlled trials that use innovative designs and electronic health records. Dr. Goldstein is currently working an international team of ethicists, healthcare providers, scientists, trialists, patient partners, and members of the public to co-develop and disseminate guidance for the ethical design and conduct of cluster randomized trials. Dr. Goldstein received a B.A. in philosophy from McGill University, and an M.A. and Ph.D. in philosophy from Western University.

speakers.name: Misty Fish
speakers.title: Dr.
speakers.bio:
Dr Misty Fish is a lawyer with experience in research integrity, administrative and public interest disclosure investigations. Misty is the Manager of the Research Integrity Office at Curtin University and has previously worked as the Senior Manager Research Integrity at the University of Queensland. Misty is responsible for managing Research Integrity complaint matters and manages the Human and Animal Ethics teams. Misty’s role also involves oversight of Curtin’s Animal Ethics and Human Research Ethics Committees. 
With a PhD in law focussing on wild animal welfare and environmental law, Misty is well versed in animal welfare and conservation protections. Misty also has significant experience in external administrative review, having worked at the Queensland Ombudsman which included review of government agencies, correctional facilities, and universities, to ensure proper, transparent and accountable administrative process and governance.

speakers.name: De Ming Chau
speakers.title: Dr.
speakers.bio: Dr. De Ming Chau is a molecular biologist with experience in cancer stem cell research. He is the co-founder of the Young Scientists Network-Academy of Sciences Malaysia (YSN-ASM) Responsible Conduct of Research (RCR) Programme and he has been chairing the YSN-ASM Science Integrity Working Group since 2017. Dr. Chau is the co-editor of the Malaysian Educational Module on RCR and a contributor of the Malaysian Code of Responsible Conduct in Research. Currently, Dr. Chau is a committee member of the National Committee on Research Integrity (Malaysia) and the Co-Chair of the Association of Southeast Nations (ASEAN) RCR Project.

speakers.name: Natalie Evans
speakers.title: Dr
speakers.bio: Natalie Evans is an Associate Professor in the Department of Ethics, Law and Humanities at Amsterdam UMC. Her work explores how responsible research practices are conceptualized and embedded within academic communities, with a focus on virtue ethics, fairness, equity, and environmental responsibility in research conduct. She has played a leading role in major European research integrity projects, including RE4GREEN, PREPARED, AIOLIA, VIRT2UE, and EnTIRE. As Scientific Coordinator of VIRT2UE, she oversaw the development of a virtue ethics–based training program that prepared nearly 500 trainers and reached over 4,000 researchers across Europe. speakers.name: Fred Yao Gbagbo
speakers.title: Prof.
speakers.bio: I am a Public Health Consultant with specialization in Population and Health, Sexual Reproductive Health and Rights (SRHR), Bioethics, Health Policy Analysis, and Reviews. I have 25 years of cumulative work experience in public, private and NGO Health sectors in Ghana and internationally. I have served on several boards/committees in various capacities and collaborated with multiple institutions/teams on SRHR projects. My research outputs have contributed significantly to informing SRHR policy and program development in Ghana and beyond. I am currently an Associate Professor at the University of Education, Winneba, Ghana.

presentations.theme: Research integrity and research ethics structures

speakers.name: Carl Taswell
speakers.title: Dr.
speakers.bio: Dr. Taswell has been certified as a diplomate of both the American Board of Nuclear Medicine and the American Board of Psychiatry & Neurology, and serves on the faculty at UCSD with expertise in functional and molecular brain imaging. He received his BA in biochemistry from Harvard College, his MSc in mathematics and MD in medicine both from New York University, and his PhD in scientific computing and computational mathematics from Stanford University. He has completed a post-doctoral fellowship in biostatistics at Harvard School of Public Health, as well as clinical training in psychiatry at UCSD and in nuclear medicine at UCSF. Since receiving a top ten scholarship award in the 1974 Science Talent Search (administered by the Society for Science), he has published numerous research papers spanning the fields of biochemistry, immunology, medicine, statistics, mathematics, informatics and brain imaging.

speakers.name: Zexing Song
speakers.title: Dr.
speakers.bio:
Dr. Zexing Song is a dermatologist and PhD student in Clinical Epidemiology at
the Institute of Health Policy, Management, and Evaluation, University of Toronto. His research focuses on developing clinical prediction models, assessing the risk of bias in observational studies, and promoting open science practices to enhance transparency and reproducibility in clinical research

speakers.name: Bert Seghers
speakers.title: Mr.
speakers.bio: Bert Seghers, mathematician by training, coordinates the Flemish Commission for Research Integrity, the second-advice body for research integrity complaints in Dutch-speaking Belgium. He is the President of ENRIO, the European Network of Research Integrity Offices.

speakers.name: Viktor Scholz
speakers.title: Dr.
speakers.organization: Ombudsgremium Für Die Wissenschaftliche Integrität In Deutschland
speakers.bio: Viktor Scholz holds a Ph.D. in Islamic Studies and supports the Ombuds Committee for Research Integrity as deputy head of office. Before, he was scientific coordinator for research quality at the Berlin University Alliance, where he developed strategies and services to advance research quality and open science in the Berlin research area and a multimedia E-learning course on good research practice for doctoral researchers. Earlier, he developed new models for the professionalisation of Berlin ombuds systems, e.g. via workshops and a Toolbox, in the BUA-funded project „OBUA – Ombudswesen@BUA“ (2021-2023) together with project leader Simona Olivieri.

speakers.name: Anu Tammeleht
speakers.title: Dr.
speakers.bio: Dr Anu Tammeleht is a postdoctoral researcher at the University of Helsinki. She is also a research fellow in ethics and a methodologist/trainer of research ethics at the Centre for Ethics, University of Tartu, Estonia. She is a designer of research ethics and integrity training resources and a facilitator of trainings of different formats at higher education institutions in Estonia and at the University of Helsinki. Her research area involves development of ethics competencies and evaluating the effectiveness of research ethics and integrity training. She also contributes to developing the national research ethics and integrity system (Estonia) through national surveys, policy recommendations and presentations.

speakers.name: Andrea Tricco
speakers.title: Dr.
speakers.bio: Andrea Tricco holds an MSc in Epidemiology and a PhD in Population Health. She is a Scientist and Executive Director at the Li Ka Shing Knowledge Institute, St. Michael’s Hospital, and a Professor at the University of Toronto’s Dalla Lana School of Public Health and Institute of Health Policy, Management & Evaluation. She also serves as Adjunct and Associate Graduate Faculty at Ontario Tech University. Her research focuses on advancing knowledge synthesis to support decision-makers, leading projects on rapid reviews, network meta-analysis, and scoping reviews. Dr. Tricco has over 420 journal publications and holds a Tier 1 Canada Research Chair in Knowledge Synthesis for Knowledge Users. Recognized as a highly cited researcher, she directs an online knowledge synthesis course completed by over 655 participants. She is also Co-Editor in Chief of the Journal of Clinical Epidemiology.

speakers.name: Masaki Nakamura
speakers.title: Dr.
speakers.bio: Dr. Masaki Nakamura is a Professor at the University of Osaka, Japan, and a member of the Science Council of Japan. He received his PhD from the University of Tokyo in 2005. After serving as a research associate at the University of Tokyo’s Research Center for Advanced Science and Technology and as a research fellow at the National Institute of Science and Technology Policy (MEXT), he joined the University of Osaka in 2007 and was promoted to professor in 2021. He also serves on the board of the Association for the Promotion of Research Integrity (APRIN), Japan’s core national organization promoting research integrity. His research covers a wide range of topics, including research integrity, science communication, and the history of science and technology.

speakers.name: Amanda Montoya
speakers.title: Dr.
speakers.bio: I am an Associate Professor at UCLA in the Department of Psychology - Quantitative Area. I received my PhD in Quantitative Psychology from the Ohio State University in 2018. My primary adviser was Dr. Andrew Hayes. I completed my M.A. in Psychology and my M.S. in Statistics at Ohio State in 2016. I graduated from the University of Washington with a B.S. in Psychology and a minor in Mathematics in 2013. My research interests include mediation, moderation, conditional process models, structural equation modeling, and meta-science.

speakers.name: Marcel Martončik
speakers.title: Dr.
speakers.organization: Institute of Social Sciences CSPS SAS, Slovakia
speakers.bio: Marcel Martončik (PhD) is interested in how to increase the credibility and replicability of behavioral research and seeks to promote these approaches as co-founder of the Slovak Reproducibility Network. His main research focus is gaming disorder, which he investigates as part of the ERC ORE longitudinal project aimed at building a multicultural understanding of how people play videogames at different intensity levels. He runs an initiative aimed at improving access to support for people in Slovakia with gaming disorder. His work also covers esports and human performance, particularly examining which psychological factors are important for success in different esports games.

speakers.name: Ana Marušić
speakers.title: Prof.
speakers.organization: University Of Split School Of Medicine
speakers.bio: Ana Marušić is Professor of Anatomy, Chair of the Department of Research in Biomedicine and Health, and Head of the Center for Evidence-based Medicine at the University of Split School of Medicine. Her research interests include evidence-based medicine and research integrity. She has published over 400 articles in peer-reviewed journals and was heavily involved with creating the policy of mandatory registration of clinical trials in public registries which helped change the legal regulation of clinical trials worldwide. Prof. Marušić is the founder of Cochrane Croatia and also serves on the Advisory Board the EQUATOR Network. She was the editor in chief of the Croatian Medical Journal and the Journal of Global Health. She is currently the Senior Editor-in-Chief of ST-OPEN, an overlay journal of the University of Split. She serves on the Council of the Committee on Publication Ethics (COPE), and as the president of The Embassy Foundation. She is the founding member of the Croatian Reproducibility and Integrity Network, CroRIN.

speakers.name: Gráinne McNamara
speakers.title: Dr.
speakers.bio: Gráinne leads the Research Integrity / Publication Ethics team at Karger Publishers, responsible for developing research integrity policies and conducting investigations for all Karger publications. She completed her PhD in Integrative Neuroscience at Cardiff University in the UK and then spent several years as a researcher at Cardiff University and Imperial College London. Since 2017 she has been working in research integrity teams at publishers and she established the research integrity / publication ethics team at Karger Publishers in 2021. In this role, she is particularly interested in research integrity promoting activities that enhance transparency and the reproducibility of research.

speakers.name: Rachael Gooberman-Hill
speakers.title: Prof.
speakers.position: Co-chair, UK Committee on Research Integrity
speakers.bio: Rachael is Co-chair of the UK Committee on Research Integrity, and is Professor of Health and Anthropology at the University of Bristol. She has spent most of her career to date working in interdisciplinary research. Rachael has served on research advisory groups, steering groups, funding panels, and as a trustee and committee member of scholarly associations.

speakers.name: Panagiotis Kavouras
speakers.title: Dr.
speakers.bio: Panagiotis Kavouras is a researcher at the Institute of Health and Society, Centre for Medical Ethics, Faculty of Medicine, University of Oslo and at the Association of Hellenic Academic Libraries, at the Center for Libraries and Informatics at the Aristotle University of Thessaloniki. For almost two decades, his research interests focused on materials technology. Since 2017, his research interests focused on Research Integrity. He has been involved in many EU-funded projects, related to Research Integrity and Research Ethics. Currently, he is the scientific coordinator of the TRUSTparency project. He is member of the program committee of the ENRIO Congresses on Research Integrity Practice and he was member of the local organizing committee of the 8th World Conference on Research Integrity. In addition to his academic track record, he has written extensively in the Greek language, mainly on issues related to Research Integrity, in a magazine for science, technology and society.

speakers.name: Liz Jackson
speakers.title: Dr.
speakers.bio: Liz Jackson is Karen Lo Eugene Chuang Professor in Diversity and Equity at the University of Hong Kong and the Associate Dean (Research) of its Faculty of Education. She is President of the Comparative Education Society of Hong Kong and Editor-in-Chief of Educational Philosophy and Theory. She is the author of over 200 books, journal articles, and book chapters, including  Beyond Virtue: The Politics of Educating Emotions, Questioning Allegiance: Resituating Civic Education and Emotions: Philosophy of Education in Practice.

speakers.name: Evan Laye
speakers.title: Mr.
speakers.bio: Evan Laye was born in Winnipeg, MB and raised by his parents, Sheldon and Elaine, in his home community of Selkirk, MB.  Evan is Red River Michif and has relations across Manitoba, with most of his family located in the Interlake and Northern regions of the province.  He has lived in various locations across Canada, including Saskatchewan, Ontario, British Columbia, and remote northern Manitoba.  Evan’s childhood experiences on the land inspired an interest in the natural world, which shaped his academic and professional careers.  Evan has received training in environmental science from Western academic institutions, completing a Bachelor of Environmental Science at the University of Manitoba (2015) and a Masters in Environmental Science at the University of British Columbia (2024), alongside Indigenous teachings from Elders, Knowledge Keepers, and cultural leaders with whom he has built relationships with over time.  As the NRC’s first Indigenous Scholar in Residence, Evan will continue his research training while completing a PhD in Indigenous Knowledge at the University of British Columbia.

speakers.name: Carl Walter Matthias Kaiser
speakers.title: Prof.
speakers.bio: Matthias Kaiser is Professor Emeritus at the Center for the Study of the Sciences and Humanities (SVT), University of Bergen, and Senior Researcher at the Norwegian Institute for Sustainability Studies (NORSUS), having studied at the universities of Munich, Oslo, Stanford and Frankfurt. He is also an Affiliated Fellow at the Koi Tū: Centre for Informed Futures, University of Auckland, and Editor-in-Chief of the Springer journal Food Ethics. Areas of expertise: philosophy of science (Dr.phil.), ethics of science, food ethics, technology assessment and science-for-policy.

speakers.name: Takehito Kamata
speakers.title: Dr
speakers.bio: Takehito Kamata, Ph.D., is an Assistant Professor by Special Appointment at Sophia University in Tokyo, Japan. He was a fellow in the inaugural Japan-U.S. Science Communication & Policy Fellowship Network Program and received the Distinguished International Alumni Award from the University of Minnesota College of Education and Human Development in 2023. Currently, he serves as Vice Chair of the International Exchange Committee (2024-2026) at the Association for the Promotion of Research Integrity in Japan. In 2026, he was a Japan Chair Visiting Fellow at the Hudson Institute, conducting research on research security from January to February, with the Cabinet Office of Japan’s endorsement. His research interests encompass international research collaborations, research integrity, research security, higher education internationalization, and related subjects in science, technology, and innovation policy within the context of higher education.

speakers.name: Shawn McGuirk
speakers.title: Mr.
speakers.organization: Natural Sciences And Engineering Research Council Of Canada (NSERC)
speakers.bio: Shawn McGuirk is Director for Research Security at the Natural Sciences and Engineering Research Council of Canada (NSERC), where his team coordinates the harmonized implementation of research security measures at NSERC and across the Canadian federal granting agencies, working closely with Canada’s Research Security Centre and other federal partners. Shawn also represents NSERC as co-chair of the Global Research Council’s Responsible Research Assessment Working Group. He joined NSERC in 2018 as a Canadian Science Policy Fellow and is an alumnus of the Government of Canada’s Recruitment of Policy Leaders program. Previously, he conducted research at the Goodman Cancer Research Institute of McGill University centered on the role of cellular metabolism in cancer progression.

speakers.name: Natalie Collins

presentations.title: Integrating Research Security and Research Integrity in International Research Collaborations: Engaged Institutional Policy Development in Japan

speakers.name: Takehito Kamata
speakers.title: Dr
speakers.bio: Takehito Kamata, Ph.D., is an Assistant Professor by Special Appointment at Sophia University in Tokyo, Japan. He was a fellow in the inaugural Japan-U.S. Science Communication & Policy Fellowship Network Program and received the Distinguished International Alumni Award from the University of Minnesota College of Education and Human Development in 2023. Currently, he serves as Vice Chair of the International Exchange Committee (2024-2026) at the Association for the Promotion of Research Integrity in Japan. In 2026, he was a Japan Chair Visiting Fellow at the Hudson Institute, conducting research on research security from January to February, with the Cabinet Office of Japan’s endorsement. His research interests encompass international research collaborations, research integrity, research security, higher education internationalization, and related subjects in science, technology, and innovation policy within the context of higher education.

presentations.title: Recommendations to Make the Current Research Integrity Framework More Relevant to Processes and Stakeholders in Innovation and Policy Making: The Athens Statement

speakers.name: Panagiotis Kavouras
speakers.title: Dr.
speakers.bio: Panagiotis Kavouras is a researcher at the Institute of Health and Society, Centre for Medical Ethics, Faculty of Medicine, University of Oslo and at the Association of Hellenic Academic Libraries, at the Center for Libraries and Informatics at the Aristotle University of Thessaloniki. For almost two decades, his research interests focused on materials technology. Since 2017, his research interests focused on Research Integrity. He has been involved in many EU-funded projects, related to Research Integrity and Research Ethics. Currently, he is the scientific coordinator of the TRUSTparency project. He is member of the program committee of the ENRIO Congresses on Research Integrity Practice and he was member of the local organizing committee of the 8th World Conference on Research Integrity. In addition to his academic track record, he has written extensively in the Greek language, mainly on issues related to Research Integrity, in a magazine for science, technology and society.

speakers.name: Liz Jackson
speakers.title: Dr.
speakers.bio: Liz Jackson is Karen Lo Eugene Chuang Professor in Diversity and Equity at the University of Hong Kong and the Associate Dean (Research) of its Faculty of Education. She is President of the Comparative Education Society of Hong Kong and Editor-in-Chief of Educational Philosophy and Theory. She is the author of over 200 books, journal articles, and book chapters, including  Beyond Virtue: The Politics of Educating Emotions, Questioning Allegiance: Resituating Civic Education and Emotions: Philosophy of Education in Practice.

presentations.title: Integrity of Science in Collision With Political Interest: A Challenge in Collaborative Research With Indigenous Communities

speakers.name: Carl Walter Matthias Kaiser
speakers.title: Prof.
speakers.bio: Matthias Kaiser is Professor Emeritus at the Center for the Study of the Sciences and Humanities (SVT), University of Bergen, and Senior Researcher at the Norwegian Institute for Sustainability Studies (NORSUS), having studied at the universities of Munich, Oslo, Stanford and Frankfurt. He is also an Affiliated Fellow at the Koi Tū: Centre for Informed Futures, University of Auckland, and Editor-in-Chief of the Springer journal Food Ethics. Areas of expertise: philosophy of science (Dr.phil.), ethics of science, food ethics, technology assessment and science-for-policy.

speakers.name: Evan Laye
speakers.title: Mr.
speakers.bio: Evan Laye was born in Winnipeg, MB and raised by his parents, Sheldon and Elaine, in his home community of Selkirk, MB.  Evan is Red River Michif and has relations across Manitoba, with most of his family located in the Interlake and Northern regions of the province.  He has lived in various locations across Canada, including Saskatchewan, Ontario, British Columbia, and remote northern Manitoba.  Evan’s childhood experiences on the land inspired an interest in the natural world, which shaped his academic and professional careers.  Evan has received training in environmental science from Western academic institutions, completing a Bachelor of Environmental Science at the University of Manitoba (2015) and a Masters in Environmental Science at the University of British Columbia (2024), alongside Indigenous teachings from Elders, Knowledge Keepers, and cultural leaders with whom he has built relationships with over time.  As the NRC’s first Indigenous Scholar in Residence, Evan will continue his research training while completing a PhD in Indigenous Knowledge at the University of British Columbia.

sessions.name: Conference Dinner - A Night at the Aquarium
sessions.details:
Experience an unforgettable evening at A Night at the Aquarium, set within one of Vancouver’s most captivating venues. With exclusive after-hours access, guests will dine and connect surrounded by the breathtaking marine exhibits and immersive galleries that create a truly extraordinary backdrop.

The evening will feature a culinary journey inspired by the Pacific Northwest, highlighting fresh seafood, locally sourced ingredients, and curated British Columbia wines and craft beverages. Thoughtfully designed food stations will complement the coastal setting, offering a refined and distinctly West Coast dining experience.

Throughout the night, attendees will have the opportunity to explore select galleries, sparking natural moments of discovery and conversation in a setting unlike any other.

Blending sustainability, sophistication, and West Coast charm, A Night at the Aquarium promises a truly distinctive experience, one that is as inspiring as it is memorable.

This is a ticketed event and registration is required. Seats are limited.
Transportation to and from the venue will be provided.

sessions.name: Plenary Session #2 - GenAI and Research integrity: Global Ethics, African Wisdom, Shared Responsibility
sessions.details:
This plenary confronts a critical challenge: how GenAI is reshaping research integrity and marginalising non-Western knowledge systems. As GenAI increasingly governs what counts as legitimate scholarship, we risk creating new forms of epistemic injustice that echo colonial patterns of knowledge suppression. 
Three scholars unite to examine how algorithmic bias, data sovereignty violations, and amplified power imbalances are undermining research foundations. They explore urgent systemic challenges including knowledge distortion, epistemological colonisation, and systematic erasure. By integrating Indigenous wisdom and humanities expertise into research integrity frameworks, this session charts pathways for protecting diverse epistemological traditions whilst harnessing AI's potential responsibly.
Waddington examines how GenAI content moderation creates 'integrity gaps' by suppressing scholarly work on sensitive topics. These systems particularly marginalise knowledge that challenges dominant narratives or draws on non-Western epistemologies, with Indigenous knowledge especially vulnerable. 
Ọlọ́jẹ́dẹ́ introduces Ọmọlúwàbí virtue ethics as a research integrity model. She explores how Indigenous values can inform AI ethics, and whether current institutional frameworks can accommodate diverse moral traditions. 
Visagie presents a framework for an African-led AI research ethics code, grounded in Indigenous knowledge principles of justice in knowledge production. She highlights Africa's pivotal role in shaping integrity standards, showing how this wisdom drives global standards. 

This panel brings together diverse global perspectives to explore how GenAI is reshaping the ethics and integrity of research in ways that demand urgent attention to Indigenous epistemologies, global equity, and the safeguarding of academic freedom. Drawing on global case studies (but with a specific focus on Africa) the panel speaks directly to all three core themes of WCRI 2026: 
1. Indigenous Ways of Being 
The panel centres Indigenous knowledge systems—not only as cultural heritage to be protected, but as epistemic foundations for reshaping global integrity frameworks. Presentations draw on Yorùbá philosophy and African research ethics governance, while also highlighting how global AI systems marginalise Indigenous data and knowledge. 
2. Intersection of GenAI and Research Practices 
All three presentations critically assess how GenAI is reshaping the practice, dissemination, and governance of research. The panel challenges techno-solutionist assumptions and explores how GenAI affects academic freedom, policy formation, and knowledge equity. 
3. Research Security 
The panel explores how GenAI introduces new forms of knowledge suppression, bias, and epistemic violence, which threaten the security and integrity of research - especially for scholars and communities working on politically sensitive or culturally significant topics. 

TIME SCHEDULE
8:30am - 8:57am: GenAI, Academic Freedom, and the Integrity of Research Knowledge,  Dr Lorna Waddington
9:00am - 9:27am: Ọ OỌmoọlúwàbí and Research in AI, Dr H. Títílọolá Olojedeẹ́ 
9:30 - 9:57am: Grounding Research Integrity in Epistemic Justice: An African Path to an Indigenous Artificial Intel, Dr Retha Visagie

speakers.name: Retha Visagie
speakers.title: Dr.
speakers.bio: Dr. Retha Visagie is a certified Senior Research Management Professional (IRRC) and a leading voice in research integrity and ethics governance across Africa. With over a decade of experience, she has developed tailored training programs, policies, and systems for researchers, administrators, and ethics committees. As co-founder and co-chair of SARIMA’s Northern Regions Community of Practice, she fosters regional collaboration and best practices. She leads Unisa’s role in the Gates-funded SARIMA Capacity Strengthening Project and co-authored the SADC Research Ethics Guidelines and Nagoya Protocol Toolkit. Currently chairing the EthiXPERT Board and co-PI on the RHInnO Ethics AI Project, she champions Afrocentric, AI-driven ethics governance. A UNESCO-trained ethics educator and one of three Virt2ue trainers in South Africa, she has trained over 7,000 individuals. Her work has earned multiple awards and advanced policy reform, capacity building, and institutional ethics. She continues to supervise postgraduates and publish widely in peer-reviewed outlets.

speakers.name: Lorna Waddington
speakers.title: Dr.
speakers.bio: Dr Lorna Waddington is Associate Professor of International History at the University of Leeds and Academic Lead for Academic Integrity. Her research examines ideology, foreign policy, and atrocity in the twentieth century, including Britain's responses to genocide.

speakers.name: David Moher
speakers.title: Prof
speakers.bio: Dr. David Moher is a clinical epidemiologist, and professor of Epidemiology and Public Health, University of Ottawa. Professor Moher has been involved with the World Conference of Research Integrity for many years. He led an international team resulting in the “The Hong Kong Principles for assessing researchers: Fostering research integrity”, an output of the WCRI meeting in Hong Kong. Professor Moher spends his time trying help to improve publication science as director of the Centre for Journalology.

speakers.name: Titilola Olojede
speakers.title: Dr
speakers.bio: H. Titilola Olojede is a Lecturer and the Head of the Department of Philosophy at the National Open University of Nigeria (NOUN). Titilola works at the intersection of ethics, African philosophy, and gender philosophy. Her current research focus is on the ethics of artificial intelligence. She is a recipient of several research and travel grants. She has published relevant papers, including, Influence of GenAI on Higher Education in Nigeria, Techno-solutionism a Fact or Farce? , and Towards Africa AI Ethical Principles. She is a member of committees on ethical integration of AI into higher education.

speakers.title: Mr.
speakers.bio: Calvin Heal is a UK-based Research Fellow in Biostatistics, currently developing a tool (INSPECT-IPD) to identify problematic randomised controlled trials when individual participant data are available.

speakers.name: Lisa Häberlein
speakers.title: Ms.
speakers.bio: Lisa Häberlein is a Research Project Manager and Co-Team Leader at the European Network of Research Ethics Committees (EUREC). As a workpackage leader, she is involved in various EU-funded projects focused on Responsible Research and Innovation (RRI), Research Ethics (RE), and Research Integrity (RI), including VERITY, STRATEGIC, ROSiE, ETHNA System, and Path2Integrity. With a strong academic background in Philosophy, Interdisciplinary Ethics, and Education, Lisa has a deep research interest in moral concerns. She is actively involved in the Department of Medical Ethics and Human Rights at FAU Erlangen-Nuremberg, the Council for Research in Values and Philosophy (CRVP), and the Network of Young Ethics Experts of Medical Ethics Committees in Germany (AKEK).

speakers.name: Emily Hazlett
speakers.title: Dr.
speakers.bio: Dr. Emily Hazlett has been with the Duke University Office of Scientific Integrity since 2022. She manages the Duke Faculty and Staff Responsible Conduct of Research training program. Prior to joining DOSI, Dr. Hazlett received her PhD in Biomedical Sciences studying neuroethology and then spent several years working as a social researcher for the North Carolina state government.

speakers.name: Krista Holmes
speakers.title: Dr.
speakers.bio: Dr. Krista Holmes is passionate in her commitment to advancing research and innovation through academic, industry and community partnerships and collaboration. With extensive expertise in research ethics, she is actively engaged in policy and advocacy initiatives to establish and maintain high quality and standards for the responsible conduct of research provincially, nationally and internationally.

speakers.name: Sarah Jenkins
speakers.title: Miss
speakers.position: Senior Director, Research Integrity & Publishing Ethics
speakers.bio:
Sarah Jenkins is the Director for Research Integrity & Publishing Ethics at Elsevier.  Sarah and her team promote research integrity through policies, best practices, and education, support Elsevier’s Publishing Teams and Editors to investigate and resolve ethics cases and work with experts across Elsevier to build tools and develop processes to detect unethical practices during the manuscript submission and peer-review process. 

Sarah began her publishing career at Elsevier working with editors and learned societies in health and medical sciences.  Sarah later joined the Physical Sciences group as a Publishing Director where she focused on open access and open science through initiatives that promoted the deposition of code and software, and established links between original research articles and data journals.

speakers.name: Mohammad Hosseini
speakers.title: Dr.
speakers.bio: Dr. Mohammad Hosseini is an assistant professor at Northwestern University Feinberg School of Medicine. His research is focused on ethical implications of using artificial intelligence (AI) in research and publications, research ethics and integrity and open science. He is involved in and leads various ethics projects in national and international organizations (e.g., Association of Professional and Practical Ethics, National Information Standards Organization, National Academy of Sciences, and Global Young Academy). He teaches research ethics and integrity and responsible conduct of research at Northwestern University and various international institutions.

speakers.name: Dominique Roche
speakers.title: Dr.
speakers.organization: Social Sciences And Humanities Research Council Of Canada
speakers.bio: Dom Roche is a senior policy advisor at the Social Sciences and Humanities Research Council of Canada (SSHRC), where he leads SSHRC’s policy work on research data management and sharing. Dom completed a PhD in aquatic ecology at the Australian National University and then undertook postdocs in Switzerland and Canada. In 2021, he co-founded the Society for Open, Reliable and Transparent Ecology and Evolutionary biology (SORTEE.org) and was the society’s president in 2022. Dom has a longstanding interest in metascience, research integrity, and open science practices. In 2020, he received a three-year Marie Slodowska-Curie Global Fellowship from the European Commission to study data sharing practices in environmental science. In his role at SSHRC, he chairs the Tri-Agency working group responsible for implementing the Tri-Agency Research Data Management (RDM) Policy and he leads the implementation of the Policy’s Data Deposit Requirement for SSHRC, NSERC and CIHR.

speakers.name: Joeri Tijdink
speakers.title: Dr.
speakers.bio: Joeri Tijdink is an associate professor and principal investigator at Amsterdam UMC, location VUmc, and is affiliated with VU University in Amsterdam, the Netherlands. After studying medicine at Utrecht University (1999–2006) and specializing as a clinical psychiatrist in Amsterdam (2007–2012), he completed his PhD (2012–2015), entitled Publish & Perish: Research on Research and Researchers. His thesis focused on the impact of publication pressure on research quality and the mental health of researchers. His current research focuses on research integrity, reproducibility, research quality, mental well-being in academia, and research culture. He is involved in several national and international research projects, including the TIER2 project (www.tier2-project.eu), which explores the future(s) of reproducibility and develops tools to support reproducibility practices. He is also a principal investigator in the EU-funded TRUSTparency project, which implements promotion plans for different stakeholders to foster reproducibility practices. In addition, he leads several projects aimed at supporting a responsible research culture in diverse academic settings and studies how early career researchers can be empowered to speak up. He initiated the national Akademiethermometer survey, which investigates the mental health of academics in the Netherlands. Joeri is also the author of the book The Happy Academic – How to Thrive and Survive in Academia (2023), which offers guidance to early career researchers navigating the challenges of academic life. In his work, he consistently focuses on individual, cultural, and systemic factors that can help improve academia, with a strong emphasis on promoting mental health among researchers. Alongside his research, he continues to work as a clinical psychiatrist. In 2022, he was appointed as a member of The Young Academy of the Royal Netherlands Academy of Arts and Sciences, highlighting his commitment to improving research quality, strengthening the societal relevance of research, and fostering a more supportive academic environment for early career researchers.

speakers.name: Susan Passmore
speakers.title: Dr.
speakers.bio: Susan Racine Passmore, PhD, is an Assistant Professor at the University of Wisconsin-Madison, School of Nursing. Her research focuses on promoting the equitable distribution of the benefits of health research. As a cultural anthropologist, Dr. Passmore’s research program focuses on the construction of researcher “trustworthiness” and the minimization of structural barriers to diversity. She co-directs the Just Research Program, an educational initiative that enables research teams to develop capacity in research equity. She has also explored innovation in the engagement of underrepresented communities in research and has received funding from the National Human Genome Research Institute, as well as supplement awards from the National Institute on Aging and the Centers for Disease Control. Through this work, Dr. Passmore aims to develop innovative and practical approaches to support the inclusion of communities facing health disparities in science as a fundamental component of achieving health equity.

speakers.name: Er-Te Zheng
speakers.title: Mr.
speakers.bio: Er-Te Zheng is a PhD student at the School of Information, Journalism and Communication at the University of Sheffield. His research focuses on computational social science, science of science, scientometrics and research integrity.

speakers.name: Felicite Naah Tabala
speakers.title: Ms.
speakers.organization: Division of Health Operational research Ministry Of Public Health
speakers.bio: Naah Tabala Felicite is a public health professional and social scientist with over 12 years of experience in health research, ethics, and community engagement in Cameroon. She currently serves as Research Officer at the Ministry of Public Health’s Division of Health Operations Research, where she supports research protocol compliance, capacity building of Research Ethics Committees, post-approval monitoring, and coordination of national surveys. She has led or contributed to major projects including CAMPHIA (2015, 2024), IBBS 2023, PrEP pilot evaluations with USAID/PEPFAR, and COVID-19 research in Yaoundé. Felicite combines training in anthropology, psychology, public health, and health research ethics, and is completing a Master’s in Health Research Ethics at the University of KwaZulu-Natal. Her expertise spans ethics governance, compliance monitoring, stakeholder engagement, and community-based research. She has presented at national and international forums, published on HIV research and ethics, and facilitated workshops on informed consent and SOP harmonization.

speakers.name: Veerle Van den Eynden
speakers.title: Dr.
speakers.bio: Expert advisor in Research Data Management, with extensive expertise in data management and data sharing for the social, environmental, and health sciences, providing advice, training, policy development, and practical implementation.

speakers.name: Daniel Ucko
speakers.title: Dr.
speakers.position: Head Of Ethics And Research Integrity
speakers.bio:
Daniel Ucko is the Head of Ethics and Research Integrity at the American Physical Society (APS), where he is responsible for the publication ethics and integrity of the Physical Review journals. In this role he also shapes journal and ethics policy for the society. He has been an elected member of COPE Council since 2023. 

Before starting his career in academic publishing, he did research in condensed matter physics. He holds a PhD in Physics from University College London, UK (2001) and a PhD in Philosophy of Science from Stony Brook University, USA, on the subject of peer review, which he completed in 2020. His book: "The Subjectivities and Objectivities of Peer Review" was published by Brill in 2024.

In his role at SSHRC, he chairs the Tri-Agency working group responsible for implementing the Tri-Agency Research Data Management (RDM) Policy and he leads the implementation of the Policy’s Data Deposit Requirement for SSHRC, NSERC and CIHR.

affiliations: Social Sciences And Humanities Research Council Of Canada

presentations.title: The Impact of Policy Change on Science in the United States: A Qualitative Study of Researcher and Community Perspectives.

speakers.name: Susan Passmore
speakers.title: Dr.
speakers.bio: Susan Racine Passmore, PhD, is an Assistant Professor at the University of Wisconsin-Madison, School of Nursing. Her research focuses on promoting the equitable distribution of the benefits of health research. As a cultural anthropologist, Dr. Passmore’s research program focuses on the construction of researcher “trustworthiness” and the minimization of structural barriers to diversity. She co-directs the Just Research Program, an educational initiative that enables research teams to develop capacity in research equity. She has also explored innovation in the engagement of underrepresented communities in research and has received funding from the National Human Genome Research Institute, as well as supplement awards from the National Institute on Aging and the Centers for Disease Control. Through this work, Dr. Passmore aims to develop innovative and practical approaches to support the inclusion of communities facing health disparities in science as a fundamental component of achieving health equity.

presentations.title: Strengthening Research Security in Cameroon: A Review of Legal and Ethical Compliance Frameworks

speakers.name: Felicite Naah Tabala
speakers.title: Ms.
speakers.organization: Division of Health Operational research Ministry Of Public Health
speakers.bio: Naah Tabala Felicite is a public health professional and social scientist with over 12 years of experience in health research, ethics, and community engagement in Cameroon. She currently serves as Research Officer at the Ministry of Public Health’s Division of Health Operations Research, where she supports research protocol compliance, capacity building of Research Ethics Committees, post-approval monitoring, and coordination of national surveys. She has led or contributed to major projects including CAMPHIA (2015, 2024), IBBS 2023, PrEP pilot evaluations with USAID/PEPFAR, and COVID-19 research in Yaoundé. Felicite combines training in anthropology, psychology, public health, and health research ethics, and is completing a Master’s in Health Research Ethics at the University of KwaZulu-Natal. Her expertise spans ethics governance, compliance monitoring, stakeholder engagement, and community-based research. She has presented at national and international forums, published on HIV research and ethics, and facilitated workshops on informed consent and SOP harmonization.

affiliations: division of health operational research, Ministry Of Public Health

speakers.name: Hella von Unger
speakers.title: Prof.
speakers.bio: Hella von Unger is Professor of Sociology with a focus on Qualitative Methodologies at Ludwig-Maximilians-Universität München (LMU Munich), Germany. She studied Social Sciences at the University of Hannover and the University of California, Berkeley, and received her PhD in Sociology from the University of Hannover in 2005. Her dissertation – a qualitative study on multiply stigmatized lesbian and bisexual mothers living with HIV/AIDS – was funded by the HIV Center for Clinical and Behavioral Studies at Columbia University, New York. Before her appointment at LMU Munich in 2013, she held academic positions at Columbia University, the University of Magdeburg, and the Social Science Research Center Berlin (WZB), Germany. Her research focuses on qualitative and participatory social research, the sociology of health, migration studies, and research ethics. She is an elected speaker of neks (Network of Research Ethics Committees in the Social Sciences) and a partner in the EU-funded CHANGER project.

speakers.name: Anné Verhoef
speakers.title: Prof.
speakers.position: Director Of The Nwu Ai Hub
speakers.bio: Anné H. Verhoef is a professor in Philosophy and Director of the North-West University (NWU) AI Hub. He holds a PhD from the University of Stellenbosch and a second one from the Free University in Amsterdam. His research interests are, amongst others, ethics, artificial intelligence and academic integrity, and the philosophy of happiness. He published various academic articles on academic integrity in Higher Education. He is the co-founder and former associate editor of the academic journal Transformation in Higher Education. He is also the co-founder of AICSA – the Artificial Intelligence Circle in South Africa for Higher Education.

speakers.name: Marijke Van Vlasselaer
speakers.title: Ms.
speakers.bio:
Marijke Van Vlasselaer (marijke.van.vlasselaer@vub.be; ORCID: 0009-0001-2434-3695) is a 
doctoral researcher at the Data Analytics Lab, Vrije Universiteit Brussel. Her research focuses on 
academic integrity, generative AI in higher education, and policy reform in higher education. She also 
works on moral emotions, the philosophy of (alternative) education, and the didactics of philosophy.

speakers.name: Lesley Uttley
speakers.title: Dr.
speakers.bio: Lesley is a Senior Research Fellow at the School of Medicine and Population Health, University of Sheffield. She is a career development award holder (2020-2024) and transition support award holder (2025-2026) for investigating human influences in systematic reviews and meta-analyses, funded by the UKRI Medical Research Council (MRC). Lesley leads a global research integrity initiative to monitor the problems with systematic reviews (systematicreviewlution.com). Lesley is a researcher for the ESRC funded Behavioural Research Hub (BR-UK). Lesley is also an EDI Fellow for the NIHR funded Sheffield Biomedical Research Centre (2024-2026) and an Editorial Board Member for the Journal of Clinical Epidemiology. Her work is particularly focussed on research integrity, research culture and human influences in research.

speakers.name: Nathalie Voarino
speakers.title: Dr.
speakers.bio: Nathalie is a Public Policy Project Manager for the French Office for Research Integrity. With a PhD in bioethics, she left her research on AI ethics to now support the implementation of the French research integrity policy; in conducting surveys and prospective work.

speakers.name: Tim Kersjes
speakers.title: Mr.
speakers.bio: Tim Kersjes is the Head of Research Integrity, Resolutions at Springer Nature. The Resolutions team consists of highly committed research integrity expects who support in-house editors and Editors-in-Chief in handling and resolving all types of research integrity and publication ethics issues. The cases vary from plagiarism and authorship disputes to peer review manipulation, medical-ethical concerns, data fabrication and image manipulation in submitted manuscripts and published articles. Tim has worked in publishing for over 10 years, almost 9 of which in research integrity. He has advised on thousands of research integrity cases small and large, and has written hundreds of retraction notices. He is also proud to serve as an elected trustee at the Committee on Publication Ethics (COPE).

speakers.name: Sue Russell
speakers.title: Ms.
speakers.bio:
Sue Russell is Senior Funding Policy Advisor in Funding Operations and Governance at Wellcome based in the UK, having joined in 2025. Wellcome is a global charitable foundation that works towards everyone benefitting from science’s potential to improve health and save lives. 
Sue’s role at Wellcome spans a number of funding policy areas including promoting research integrity. She sits on the signatories group for the UK Concordat for Research Integrity and has been involved in rewriting the Concordat and drafting annual statement templates required under the Concordat.
Sue joined Wellcome in 2025, following 10 years in different roles leading funding policies and in grant management roles at Cancer Research UK (CRUK). Before that, Sue spent a further 10 years at various international development funders and non-governmental organisations in programmatic and funding roles, focusing on improving health and disaster relief across many low- and middle-income countries.

sessions.name: 6.A - Symposium: The Interplay between Open Science, Transparency and Artificial Intelligence
sessions.details:
The symposium 'The interplay between Open Science, Transparency, and Artificial Intelligence' explicitly adopts an interdisciplinary approach, bringing together insights from data science, legal studies, educational research, and ethics. By integrating perspectives from diverse disciplines, we provide a comprehensive understanding of the complex interactions between Artificial Intelligence and Open Science and offer practical guidance tailored specifically to the conference’s target audiences—research institutions, policymakers, ethics committees, funding bodies, and individual researchers. With the three talks: 
1. Opportunities for AI in the Research Process: Developing Benchmarks, Assessing Replicability, and Evaluating Scholarly Content (Eileen K Clancy, COS), 
2. Challenges and Ethical Considerations: Responsible Integration of AI Tools in Open Science (Training) (Wilder&Alavi, CAU&GATE), and 
3. Copyrights for Open Science and Artificial Intelligence – a Legal Perspective (Miller, MIK), chaired by Julia Priess Buchheit, 
this symposium aims to support these stakeholders in developing robust strategies for navigating ethical, legal, and methodological challenges posed by AI, ultimately fostering a more transparent, reliable, and accountable research environment. 

SPEAKERS
Description and Relevance (Artificial Intelligence and Open Science)
Eileen K Clancy, Center for Open Science, USA, coordinates three AI-OS projects that test how AI can be leveraged in the conduct, evaluation, and replication of research. 
Title: Opportunities for AI in the Research Process: Developing Benchmarks, Assessing Replicability, and Evaluating Scholarly Content
Theoretical Background: Artificial intelligence (AI) is becoming increasingly incorporated into the research landscape. An open question arises of whether and when AI can be leveraged to evaluate and conduct research, including the potential to foster reproducibility. 
To answer that question, I will refer to three projects that reflect the diverse roles AI can play in research. The first involves developing benchmarks for AI agents tasked with replicating research and conducting peer reviews. This initiative seeks to enhance AI agent performance and explicate the processes underpinning human-led research. 

The second project assesses research claims’ credibility through an innovative open challenge. By harnessing a vast corpus of over 3,000 replications, we are training AI/ML methods to predict the replicability of research claims. This effort promises to enhance the reliability of research assessments, increasing the efficiency and accuracy of these traditionally labour-intensive tasks. 

Lastly, our Lifecycle Journal pilot reimagines scholarly communication by integrating AI-driven evaluation tools alongside human assessments. This approach diversifies the evaluation landscape and promises to elevate the standards of scholarly accountability and continuous improvement.
By showcasing these projects, we offer insights into whether AI can support the conduct, evaluation, and replication of research and start a discussion with the symposium's experts. 

Relevance: These projects demonstrate concretely how AI can enhance reproducibility, a core quality criterion of research, thus aligning directly with the central goals of RI. The outcomes support decision-makers in developing evidence-based strategies to strengthen research integrity within an increasingly AI-driven research environment.
Marie Alavi and Nicolaus Wilder, GATE Initiative, Germany, are researching how often and in what way AI is discussed in Open Science Training, answering the question of what general concepts are taught at the intersection of AI-OS. 

Title: Challenges and Ethical Considerations: Responsible Integration of AI Tools in Open Science (Training) 
Theoretical background: With the increasing use of artificial intelligence (AI) tools in research, the research community needs to critically assess how this new research approach is to be evaluated from the perspective of Open Science (OS) – with research integrity and research ethics as overarching concepts. A closer look at AI functionality and OS principles reveals a complex interplay of two distinct, and at times contradictory, logics: On one hand, AI processes openly available (and reliable) scientific data, fragmenting and re-combining it through the process of probabilistic modelling of token sequences. On the other hand, OS imposes essential requirements such as traceability, responsibility, (training) data quality, and many more. 

We, therefore, urgently need to discuss the use of AI in research and incorporate acquired knowledge in OS training to achieve reliable integration and handling of AI tools in research, while also encompassing essential OS concepts.

To address these topics, we will leverage the findings from the Open Science Learning Gate (GATE). The GATE's core feature, the GATE Service, effectively analyses how often and in what way AI is discussed in OS Training. In detail, it facilitates a reciprocal exchange of knowledge between the research community, educators and experts, allowing for the identification of key concepts taught at the intersection of AI and OS. Furthermore, it can generate recommendations for essential OS guiding thoughts that should be integrated into training programs, as well as outline the technical requirements that we, as advocates of responsible research practices, must uphold at this intersection. 

Relevance: Analyzing the integration of AI into OS training directly contributes to raising researchers' awareness and competency regarding ethical and integrity-related challenges. This enables research institutions to mitigate risks proactively and systematically foster responsible AI use.
Katharina Miller, Miller International Knowledge, Spain, will discuss copyright, AI, and its research implications, considering eligibility considerations in copyright acts and the approach to classifying AI training on copyrighted data as "fair use.” 

Title: Copyrights for Open Science and Artificial Intelligence – a Legal Perspective 
Theoretical Background: The intersection of Open Science (OS) and Artificial Intelligence (AI) raises complex questions about intellectual property rights, especially concerning copyright. As AI tools increasingly rely on training data that may include copyrighted content, the legal debate about the extent to which this constitutes “fair use” or permissible exceptions (especially in jurisdictions like the EU) becomes more critical.
This poses a double challenge for researchers and institutions committed to OS principles: ensuring legal compliance while maintaining openness and reproducibility. This is particularly relevant in developing AI models that support scientific discovery but rely on data that is not always freely reusable. 

The presentation will highlight the following:
How copyright frameworks affect the use of AI in research environments;
What kinds of licensing models and legal strategies can be aligned with both Open Science and responsible AI development;
And how research institutions, policymakers, and training programs can be supported in navigating this tension through practical guidance and updated OS-AI training content. 

Relevance: Clarifying copyright issues is crucial for safeguarding integrity and ensuring legal certainty in AI-supported research. This presentation provides actionable guidance for research institutions and individual researchers to avoid potential legal infringements that could have integrity-related repercussions systematically. 

TIME SCHEDULE
10:30am - 10:35am: Opening and Introduction - Dr. Julia Priess Buchheit 
10:35am - 10:53am: Opportunities for AI in the Research Process: Developing Benchmarks, Assessing Replicability, and Evaluating Scholarly Content - Eileen K Clancy 
10:53am - 11:11am: Challenges and Ethical Considerations: Responsible Integration of AI Tools in Open Science (Training) - Nicolaus Wilder 
11:11am - 11:29am: Challenges and Ethical Considerations - Marie Alavi 
11:29am - 11:47am: Copyrights for Open Science and Artificial Intelligence – a Legal Perspective - Katharina Miller

speakers.name: Marie Alavi
speakers.title: Mrs.
speakers.bio:
Marie Alavi is a research associate at Kiel University. Her research focus is the intersection of RCR, Open Science, Intellectual Property and AI. She is the co-developer of the GATE and member of NERQ.

Marie is currently involved in the IP4OS project and GATE initiative. Prior to that, she was involved with Path2Integrity and HAnS, conducting research on research integrity and the role of AI in higher education.

speakers.name: Katharina Miller
speakers.title: Mrs.
speakers.bio:
Katharina Miller is founding partner of the boutique consultancy firm 3C Compliance and the multidisciplinary law firm Miller International Knowledge.She is a qualified lawyer in Germany and Spain with over 15 years of international practice across Western Europe. She has strong expertise in German and Spanish Corporate Governance and in the interface between Compliance & Ethics and business. Also beyond her professional activities, she is regularly consulted in ESG, CSR, and Corporate Compliance matters, for example on EU level.

Furthermore, she is a committed Non-Executive Member of various advisory and supervisory boards across industries and countries. Here, she contributes especially with legal, operational and risk management experience and in the fields ESG, Women Rights and Innovation & Technology worldwide.

Her professional activities are strongly related to the implementation of the Agenda 2030, because for her it's of utmost importance to find solutions for our living together in the "The Fourth Industrial Revolution" by implementing the 17 Sustainable Development Goals of the United Nations (SGDs).

speakers.name: Julia Priess Buchheit
speakers.title: Prof.
speakers.bio:
Julia Priess-Buchheit (Prof. Dr. phil.) is an expert in learning open science, ethics, integrity, and social technologies. She leads the Zentrum für Konstruktive Erziehungswissenschaft and conducts her research at Kiel University. Before this transition, she was a professor of Education and Didactics at the University of Applied Sciences Coburg (Academic Centre for Sciences and Humanities), implementing an interdisciplinary study program for all faculties. In 2020, she became the Dean of Studies at the Academic Centre for Sciences and Humanities and founded the (German-speaking) Teaching and Learning Scientific Practice network.
She coordinates IP4OS, a HorizonEurope project on Open Science and Intellectual Property (€ 2 million). She is one of the few coordinators of a Horizon2020 project (Path2Integrity, € 2.5 million) associated with a university of applied sciences in Germany (and Europe).
The University of Kiel awarded her the Innovative and Trendsetting Teaching prize; she won the Genius Loci-Preis with the Coburger Weg team. Her digital learning settings for students and pupils are open source, and she won both the #WeForSchool Hackathon (Germany) and the #EUvsVirus Hackathon.

speakers.name: Eileen Clancy
speakers.title: Dr.
speakers.bio: Certified medical publication professional (CMPP) with proven track record of success over 8 years in managing and leading teams, developing journal policies, and executing tactical plans to deliver robust publication pipelines while enforcing best practices and ethics standards in publishing and research.

speakers.name: Nicolaus Wilder
speakers.title: Dr.
speakers.bio: Nicolaus Wilder works as a research assistant at the Institute of Pedagogy at Kiel University in the Department of General Pedagogy. His research focuses on the theories and basic concepts of (general) pedagogy as well as its philosophical foundations and references.
